---
title: "Rose Rush (Lygodesmia aphylla)"
slug: "lygodesmia-aphylla"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622168426/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Rose Rush"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Asterales"
tax_family: "Asteraceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Asterales", "Asteraceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622168426/medium.jpg" alt="Rose Rush" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/jsfrancis">
jsfrancis</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/341881074">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Asterales
-   **Family:** Asteraceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=126341)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in dry, sandy habitats such as scrub, sandhills, and open disturbed areas.

## Natural history notes

A slender, branching wildflower characterized by its mostly leafless stems and pink to rose-colored, dandelion-like flower heads. In Florida, it is found in dry, sandy habitats such as scrub, sandhills, and open disturbed areas. It grows during the warmer months and is often a sparse, subtle component of groundcover vegetation in open, sun-exposed sites.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/126341-Lygodesmia-aphylla)
-   [Local profile](https://www.flawildflowers.org/flower-friday-lygodesmia-aphylla/)
